article_meta.title: Former President Donald Trump speaks at a campaign rally on April 27, 2023, in Manchester, New Hampshire

raw_response: {"detections":[{"biasType":"Subjective Qualifying Adjectives","biasedText":"In each version of the dramatic story","explanation":"The author uses the adjective 'dramatic' to characterize Trump's story, which is a subjective editorial judgment that frames the claims as sensational before the fact-checking even begins."},{"biasType":"Word Choice","biasedText":"Cheung sent us links to news articles that were not proof","explanation":"Describing the articles flatly as 'not proof' rather than something more neutral like 'articles the campaign said supported the claim' is a blunt editorial judgment in the author's voice that frames the response dismissively."},{"biasType":"Word Choice","biasedText":"Breitbart's vague and unverified claim","explanation":"The adjectives 'vague and unverified' applied to Breitbart's reporting are editorial characterizations by the author that go beyond neutral description."},{"biasType":"Word Choice","biasedText":"right-wing website Breitbart News","explanation":"Labeling Breitbart as 'right-wing' without similarly labeling other cited organizations (e.g., CNN itself, or the pro-immigration organizations) reveals selective ideological labeling."},{"biasType":"Spin","biasedText":"the campaign also failed to provide any evidence that South American countries are emptying mental health facilities to somehow send patients into the US","explanation":"The word 'somehow' is dismissive and editorializing, implying the mechanism described by Trump is implausible or absurd, rather than simply reporting that no evidence was provided."},{"biasType":"Spin","biasedText":"late Cuban despot Fidel Castro","explanation":"While Castro is widely considered a dictator, the word 'despot' is a loaded editorial choice by the author rather than a neutral descriptor like 'leader' or 'dictator.'"},{"biasType":"Slant","biasedText":"As a last resort, we posted Trump's quotes on Twitter and invited the public to try to find support for them. More than 30 hours later, nobody had.","explanation":"Ending the article on this note — a crowdsourced search yielding nothing after 30 hours — is framed for maximum rhetorical effect, structurally emphasizing the conclusion that no evidence exists rather than presenting this as one data point among many."}]}

detections.biasedText: In each version of the dramatic story
detections.explanation: The author uses the adjective 'dramatic' to characterize Trump's story, which is a subjective editorial judgment that frames the claims as sensational before the fact-checking even begins.

detections.biasedText: Cheung sent us links to news articles that were not proof
detections.explanation: Describing the articles flatly as 'not proof' rather than something more neutral like 'articles the campaign said supported the claim' is a blunt editorial judgment in the author's voice that frames the response dismissively.
The adjectives 'vague and unverified' applied to Breitbart's reporting are editorial characterizations by the author that go beyond neutral description.
Labeling Breitbart as 'right-wing' without similarly labeling other cited organizations (e.g., CNN itself, or the pro-immigration organizations) reveals selective ideological labeling.

detections.biasedText: the campaign also failed to provide any evidence that South American countries are emptying mental health facilities to somehow send patients into the US
detections.explanation: The word 'somehow' is dismissive and editorializing, implying the mechanism described by Trump is implausible or absurd, rather than simply reporting that no evidence was provided.
While Castro is widely considered a dictator, the word 'despot' is a loaded editorial choice by the author rather than a neutral descriptor like 'leader' or 'dictator.'

detections.biasedText: As a last resort, we posted Trump's quotes on Twitter and invited the public to try to find support for them. More than 30 hours later, nobody had.
detections.explanation: Ending the article on this note — a crowdsourced search yielding nothing after 30 hours — is framed for maximum rhetorical effect, structurally emphasizing the conclusion that no evidence exists rather than presenting this as one data point among many.
